data.title: Playboy: Asset-Light Pivot Drives Upside
data.description:
This marks my initiation of coverage on Playboy Inc. (PLBY). While I typically avoid the lifestyle brands because they often trade on opaque royalty structures that mask deteriorating core fundamentals, PLBY's recent price action, sparked by a massive $122 million China liquidity event, demands a revaluation of the stock. PLBY surged over 40% on the China news last week.

Playboy has moved from being just a media company, having shredded high overhead segments like physical publishing, and has pivoted to become an IP (intellectual property) licensing firm that monetizes its IP through long‑term trademark and digital deals.

Though Playboy has been (and I believe is still) a household name, a little background before we go into the other details. Playboy was founded in 1953 as a print and media company, best known for Playboy magazine, which was famous for adult photography and cultural interviews. The brand expanded into TV, clubs, apparel, and licensing of its bunny logo over decades. Its global recognition made it one of the most iconic lifestyle brands in the world.

PLBY has been an iconic name in global lifestyle branding, but it became a SPAC gone wrong after the de-SPAC transaction in 2021 and has since been a suppressed small cap..

Playboy is now in the final stages of a painful transition from a messy, capital-intensive media conglomerate to a high-margin, asset-light licensing business. They’ve shed the unprofitable big swing projects to focus on three cores which are: licensing its global IP, direct-to-consumer sales, and digital subscription content offerings.

From Media to Royalties: How Playboy Monetizes Its Brand

Before considering investing in PLBY, investors must understand that the brand itself is the most important asset (intangible) for Playboy Inc.

Playboy’s rabbit head logo (seen above) is the most popular of the company's IP and is its golden goose and anchor of its licensing strategy. Monetization around the brand spans apparel, accessories, gaming, hospitality, and digital content, which are supported by global opportunities in licensing these categories to third parties who create direct-to-consumer products based on the brand, digital subscriptions linked to premium creator content derived from the Playboy name as well as selling archived materials that revolve around the brand.

To further understand Playboy's business structure, investors need to separate what Playboy owns from what it licenses. In the context of today’s Playboy Inc, Honey Birdette is the main wholly owned consumer brand asset. Playboy acquired the luxury lingerie brand just after the SPAC in 2021.

Through this operating line, Playboy directly controls a premium lingerie and sexual wellness brand. It also operates physical retail stores and a global e-commerce platform, generating direct-to-consumer revenue with full control over pricing, inventory, and margins rather than purely through royalty streams.

The other digital segments are no longer fully operated in-house. In late 2024, PLBY entered a long-term licensing and management agreement with Luxembourg-based Byborg Enterprises, a large-scale online entertainment platform operator. It is a 15-year agreement under which Byborg now operates Playboy’s legacy digital operating assets like Playboy Plus, Playboy TV digital, and related creator platforms, while Playboy shifts into a licensor role. The deal structure includes a $20 million annual minimum guarantee ($5 million a quarter), totaling $300 million over the 15-year term, with potential upside royalties above that. Which converts a previously volatile, cost heavy digital operation into a more predictable contractual revenue stream, reinforcing Playboy’s pivot toward an asset-light licensing model while reducing operational risk and supporting balance sheet stabilization.

Playboy’s Operating Leverage: The Transition from Recovery to Compounding

In a forward-looking context, the margin profile of Playboy has now moved beyond that of a recovery story. The business is shifting into a stage where its asset-light model and structured licensing income are poised to deliver more predictable revenue and expanding margins.

Last week's surge to a 52-week high following the China joint venture is a sign of things to come, especially as software sells off and the market rotates into other higher-margin specialty consumer plays but with much less valuation bloat. Stocks like PLBY could rerate higher, and the timing of the China JV deal here is the bigger alpha generator.

If you zoom out, you'd see that PLBY has been a multi-year sideways play after the initial de-SPAC surge followed by sharp decline. To understand the current momentum, you have to acknowledge the wreckage that came before it and why market reaction last week was immense.

Since FY23, quarterly sales have been stagnant, hovering between $25 million and $35 million as Playboy has tried different ways to shed its skin the whole time. China licensing agreements were terminated at one point, leading to margin erosion, explaining why the market is so excited that China licensing is back.

Then the Honey Birdette acquisition, completed post-SPAC for $333 million in 2021 became a balance sheet overhang. By 2022, management was forced to take high write-downs ($277.2 million of non-cash impairments in Q2 2022, which remained high until Q3 2023, where $148 million in impairment charges related to write-down of goodwill, trademarks and other assets was recorded), essentially showing they had overpaid for the acquisition as the brand’s value plummeted on the books. This net loss remained materially high and dominated by impairment charges for several quarters, while debt piled up during this period with interest expense roughly double Playboy’s operating income. At one point in 2024, management was so desperate for cash that they moved Honey Birdette to discontinued operations and put it up for sale.

Playboy closed out 2023 with a final cleanup, and by late 2023 into 2024 large recurring impairment charges had largely stopped. However, Q3 2024 saw a brief return of those charges ($21.7 million in impairment charges), primarily related to final valuation adjustments as management prepared to move Honey Birdette back from discontinued ops to the core business (Honey Birdette has since been retained by Playboy following quarters of financial cleanups) and to finalize the termination of their license agreement in China.

The roots of the China termination stem from a dispute at the time with a Hong Kong-based licensee, known as New Handong Investment (Guangdong) Co. Ltd., because the licensee was in repeated material breaches of the licensing contracts. Playboy initiated arbitration against what was then its largest regional partner and ultimately won an $81 million award after the licensee failed to meet guaranteed minimum royalty payments.

Playboy's temporary China exit caused a long overhang. And the overhang from this era and the frustration investors have endured due to the China disruption tie directly to why the market reaction was so aggressive last week, with shares jumping ~40% on the UTG news; though there has been a retracement since last week's high around $2.70 and shares now trade around $2. And the central idea for this piece is to show if the current retracement is an attractive setup for a likely near-term bounce.

The market realizes that the UTG transaction effectively closes the bad chapter on the prior Asia disruption that followed the $81 million arbitration win, and the deal is much better than the previous as it ultimately reshapes how Playboy monetizes China moving forward. $9 million has already been paid as a deposit by UTG, and the initial closing of the deal (slated for March 31) will trigger the first major cash infusion. So March 31 is an important date to watch. Q4 and full FY25 results are also to be announced in mid-March. I anticipate an acceleration of debt reduction and improved guidance for the coming fiscal year on the results call. This could extend PLBY momentum. Basically, I believe with the UTG deal and recent momentum followed by the current retrace, buying into the earnings in mid March and the China deal closing by the end of March will be a well timed setup.

Details of the UTG agreement include $67 million in guaranteed minimum distribution payments from the initial close of the deal through 2033, providing a baseline contractual cash inflow to Playboy irrespective of short‑term retail performance.

Here is how the money flows between Playboy and UTG. First, Playboy sold 50% of its China licensing JV interest to UTG for $45 million in upfront cash. That immediately strengthened liquidity and reduced balance sheet pressure.

Second, the joint venture now controls the Playboy brand across mainland China and other parts of the region. UTG operates the business locally and commercializes the brand locally either through licensed products in the region or by entering into sub-licensing arrangements.

Third, under the agreement, UTG committed to $67 million in guaranteed minimum distributions to Playboy through 2033. These function similarly to minimum royalty guarantees. If the business underperforms, UTG still owes the floor payment. If it outperforms, Playboy participates through its 50% equity interest in the JV’s profits.

So Playboy now benefits in two ways: contracted minimum distributions that provide baseline cash flow, and 50% participation in upside economics through JV profit sharing

These are materially different from the prior structure with the former licensee, which was a straightforward trademark license. Playboy granted IP rights and relied on the counterparty to generate sales and honor guaranteed minimum royalty payments. When those guarantees were not met, the economics collapsed and the litigation followed.

The UTG structure aligns incentives as UTG is not only a licensee but also a co-owner of the regional business. As a 50% partner of the JV, UTG has equity at risk and operational responsibility. Performance now directly impacts both parties.

The Reset on the Ground: Playboy's Deleveraging Plan

While the structure of the China deal gives Playboy a fresh framework to generate durable cash flows, actions management is taking to clean up remnants of the years of overleverage are just as important as the deal itself.

I've mentioned how debt ballooned over the years during the period of aggressive acquisitions and heavy writedowns. As of Q3 2025 end, Playboy carried ~$175 million in total debt, while net interest expense reduced to $1.9 million for the quarter (amendments to debt facility, which included interest rate reductions, in Q3 2025 have helped in this regard). I’d say FY25 was a better year for Playboy in terms of interest on debt compared to previous years, where that interest was between $5 million and $7 million a quarter, sometimes even exceeding operating income (you can verify this in the preceding income statement snapshot).

Management's commitment to further use at least $50 million of the UTG deal proceeds for debt reduction will bring the total debt load down to around $125 million from the levels it was as of Q3 end. And that invariably means further reduction in net interest expenses, and much less pressure on operating income.

Now, if you look closer at the same income statement trend, operating income came in positive in Q3, which has taken several quarters for Playboy to turn to profitability. But the interest expense ($1.9 million) still exceeded operating income, and ordinarily it would have also been a negative bottom line, but other non-operating income helped Playboy avoid a repeat bottom line negativity in Q3. And as simple as it sounds, the interest expense is now the most important lever that will determine whether Playboy can sustain profits on the bottom line moving forward.

The main insight is that other steps the company has taken on the ground (mentioned throughout this piece) set the stage for sustained breakeven and beyond. As debt is paid down with $50 million from the JV deal proceeds, interest expense is bound to reduce further. Impairments from past overpayments and write-downs are also now lower, and cash inflows from the new deal are well structured enough to push Playboy into sustained profitability.

Cashflow from the Byborg deal has also boosted Playboy’s earnings throughout 2025 as licensing revenue scaled higher due to the minimum guaranteed payments of $5 million quarterly from Byborg.

Adjusted EBITDA grew sequentially in every quarter of 2025 to reach $4.1 million in Q3. The EBITDA of $4.1 million in Q3 would have been higher if not for a $2.5 million spent in one-time legal fees. If we strip those out, Playboy’s true earnings power is already running over $6 million quarterly.

Notice the 1,200 bps jump in gross margin from 64% to 76% (in the preceding table). This is the direct impact of the Byborg deal ($20M annually, $5 million quarterly). Every licensing dollar has nearly 100% pass-through to gross profit, unlike the inventory-heavy retail sales of the former Playboy.

Buying at This Level: Positioning for PLBY Momentum Continuation

For a stock like PLBY that has been sideways for as long as most investors can remember, catching the bottom is less important than catching the move as it develops. Trading names like these tend to revolve around momentum and for how long momentum can be sustained. I believe PLBY is basically a momentum play with some touch of a turnaround story at the moment.

I mentioned earlier that March will be an important month for PLBY, as earnings for Q4 and full year 2025 will be announced in mid March. The initial closing of the UTG deal is also happening around the end of March.

Going into FY26, as guaranteed minimum distributions from the UTG JV deal and Byborg hit the books, I believe Playboy could sustain a normalized EBITDA in the range of $20 million to $25 million, since it is already hitting $6 million (stripping out one time legal costs) with operations largely stabilized.

With the $50M debt paydown from the UTG proceeds, Playboy's enterprise value would shrinks from ~$370 million at the moment to roughly $300 million, assuming cash on the balance sheet doesn't adjust downward much. Which I think will be the case here since the paydown is coming directly from the UTG deal proceeds and not from existing balance sheet cash.

At a $300 million EV and around $20 million EBITDA, PLBY will be trading at ~12x to ~15x EV/EBITDA. For a high-margin, asset-light royalty business with the propensity to convert revenue into very high margins, this is within range, though more established peers in the global consumer IP monetization and asset-light licensing revenue like Walt Disney (DIS) or Hasbro (HAS) typically trade lower, between 10x to 15x EBITDA. These companies are the top of the category in both scale, earnings visibility and free cash flow predictability. But the Buy case for Playboy is anchored in a turnaround story and much larger upside potential in China licensing, Byborg integration, and other high-margin licensing lines. Disney’s EV/EBITDA and margin metrics are a blended average across high-CapEx segments like theme parks, cruise lines, and studio content, which depress overall profitability. Playboy, in contrast, is largely an asset-light licensing and digital IP business. Revenues are mostly strictly royalty-like or contractual, with minimal overhead, so margins on its licensing lines, when things take off proper and the initial closing of the deal, has potential to be higher than Disney’s blended margins, giving PLBY the potential to trade higher.

For the near-term re-rating potential, PLBY could mirror the Lands' End (LE) precedent from last month, which saw LE rerate after the WHP Global JV. This is very similar to PLBY's setup going into the end of March initial closing of the JV deal.

Risks and Takeaway

The China JV deal is the main catalyst for all the momentum PLBY has gained lately. While other financial math is trending positive, if this deal for any reason stalls or fails to close, this would put a dent in the momentum seen so far. Like any cross-border JV, there are customary closing conditions. If regulatory hurdles in China or administrative delays, for example, push the closing date back, the market will treat it as a broken deal, likely sending the stock back to its previous $1.30–$1.50 support levels.

If it all goes as planned, Playboy’s pivot to asset-light licensing means predictable high-margin cash flow and sustained upside momentum for PLBY, making the current retracement from last week's highs a tactical entry point.

data.title: Accendra Health: Debt Reduction Creates Long-Term Opportunity (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Accendra Health, Inc. (ACH), have been a poor performer over the past year, losing over 60% of their value. The company, formerly known as Owens & Minor, has struggled with a significantly overleveraged balance sheet and narrow margins. This has forced the company to divest its legacy business and refocus just on its home care business. Still, debt concerns remain front and center. I last covered shares in December when I upgraded them to “Hold” after being a long-term bear, thinking shares were sufficiently punished. I should have just stayed negative, as the stock has lost a further 24% of its value. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit ACH.

Q4 Results Were Mixed As P&HS Sale Is Completed

In the company’s fourth quarter, Accendra Health lost $0.12 per share on a GAAP basis, but excluding one-time items, adjusted EPS was $0.21. EPS was down about 35% from last year as adjusted EBITDA declined 12% to $90 million. During the quarter, the company completed the sale of its Products & Healthcare Services business, and going forward, results will now entirely come from its home care business.

ACH’s business continues to face margin pressure. While revenue was up $14 million, or about 2%, the cost of revenue grew by $24 million, or nearly 7%. The company has struggled with the loss of a significant contract, which is reducing the run rate by ~$250+ million going forward, and that has begun to pressure margins. I expect to see the company undertake significant cost cuts in 2026 to broadly stabilize margin. During the fourth quarter, it generated $18 million of free cash flow, leaving it with $98 million for the year.

Its business primarily serves the sleep apnea and diabetes markets, which are both growing categories. In particular, sleep apnea is increasingly diagnosed, and once ACH captures a customer, there can be a long-term tail of recurring revenue, given the chronic nature of these conditions. I do see pricing pressure persisting, especially as the Federal government faces large deficits and looks to limit costs, offsetting some of this volume growth. In the long term, GLP-1 drug adoption can reduce diabetes, but this will not reduce the current population who already have the disease, which is why it is not a major near-term growth impediment in my view.

2026 Should Be A Trough Year

Alongside results, Accendra Health provided initial guidance for this year. Its outlook assumes a ~$162 million drop in revenue because it lost a large contract. ACH expects to partially offset this loss with volume growth, aided by a new agreement with UnitedHealth (UNH). After this 2026 reset, we should see a return to growth in 2027. Management expects to grow free cash flow next year. However, this outlook is excluding cash outlays to support cost reductions from removing stranded costs now that P&HS is separated. As such, “true” free cash flow is likely to be at least $40 million lower in my view.

As you can see below, the outlook implies a ~$30 million drop in EBITDA as the company faces this lower revenue base. Volume and price actions are expected to largely offset higher supplier costs, though the loss of this large customer is a significant headwind. I am a bit cautious about this outlook, as I am concerned that stranded costs from the P&HS divestiture may linger for a bit longer. As such, I see EBITDA coming in closer to $325-$340 million.

Still, even at the company’s own midpoint, it highlights that ACH is not really a significant growth company, even after divesting its lower-margin business. Plus, beyond this year, I remain concerned about potential risk from GLP-1 drug adoption, which could reduce diabetes prevalence, reducing demand for its home care products. I again emphasize that this is a long-term concern, largely beyond the 2028 timeframe. Offsetting this, sleep apnea should be a growth category. I see long-term growth in the ~3% range. This outlook nonetheless clarifies how difficult ACH’s balance sheet positioning is.

Debt Reduction Is A Multiyear Effort

Thanks to proceeds from the P&HS sale, Accendra ended the year with $282 million of cash on its balance sheet. ACH ended up receiving $342 million in Q4 for this business, and it expects to receive $14 million in Q1 as the working capital position is trued up. In total, it expects to receive $356 million, less than the preliminary $375 million announcement. Against this cash total, it is carrying $2.05 billion of debt, and we should see the company use its cash position to begin paying down debt.

Even assuming ~$70 million of further net debt reduction with free cash flow, OMI will end 2026 with about $1.7 billion of debt. This means that net leverage is likely to end this year at 4.9-5x, an extremely elevated level. On the earnings call, management reiterated its 3x leverage target, but I believe it will take multiple years and solid execution to achieve this. At its current run rate, ACH needs to reduce debt by at least $650 million. Even assuming ~$50 million of EBITDA growth over three years, it would likely not be until 2030 that leverage reaches the 3x target.

This speaks to the difficulty of OMI’s balance sheet positioning after poor M&A in prior years. The company has significant liquidity today and covenant headroom, which provides ample operating flexibility. However, barring a sharp upturn in growth (which seems unlikely), balance sheet repair will be the focus for the next several years. Even if management ultimately becomes comfortable with leverage running a bit higher (i.e., 3.5x), capital returns to shareholders are unlikely for 4 years. This is the primary argument against owning ACH—the amount of patience required to own this stock is substantial.

Shares Are Cheap Enough To Buy, But Patience Is Required

Back in December, I saw the company generating about $0.80 of free cash flow/share in 2026, rising past $1.10 in 2027 as it eliminates costs from its legacy P&HS business. My free cash flow outlook includes costs associated with removing stranded costs, which is why it is lower than the outlook. I believe my number is more relevant when considering how much cash ACH will actually generate to repay debt. I see no reason to change my outlook.

Shares today are just 2x free cash flow, which is exceptionally cheap. This speaks to how much the debt load is compressing its equity valuation. The question is what investors should pay for a stock that has a liquidity runway but will be focused on debt reduction for 4 years. In theory, even holding the enterprise value flat, ~$400 million of net debt reduction by the end of 2030 would support a ~$7 share price by then, a substantial return over 4 years. This process could elongate to 5 years, but even then, the return is still substantial.

Given its balance sheet and the long-term nature of its potential improvement, I emphasize that ACH is a speculative stock that requires a significant time horizon before investing. As such, it is not the type of stock that I would make a top holding in a portfolio, but in a diversified portfolio, there can be room for more speculative long-term plays. I think shares have become so beaten up that at 2x 2027 free cash flow, there is an opportunity brewing here.

Because of the long-term nature of the balance sheet repair, ACH shares may not recover tomorrow, but for investors with a long-term horizon, I am upgrading the stock to a “Buy.” As we see stranded costs get removed and gross debt start to come down, we should begin to see shares gradually recover and the free cash flow multiple slowly expand as the debt overhang lessens. After tremendous underperformance, there is now enough cushion to buy ACH given how cheap the valuation is.

data.title: Robert Half: Deep Value With Unusual Short Squeeze Potential
data.description:
Robert Half Inc. (RHI) the employment placement and recruiting firm, has become incredibly cheap over the past few years. Its business has soured to a minor degree with falling sales and earnings since 2022. Plus, in recent months, fears of AI disruption in RHI's third-party intermediary business model have crept into the picture. The idea is job searches are getting easier and quicker with AI aggregating data from numerous sources with a few select search words. Honestly, we have witnessed a perfect storm of problems for share pricing since 2022's all-time peak of $122.

I mentioned Robert Half's upside potential in a July 2024 article here. Unfortunately, the stock has been on a downhill roll ever since. What I did not foresee was a sustainable trend of business customers foregoing placement firms in favor of inhouse efforts to find new employees. This has been an industrywide problem, not just affecting RHI.

However, Robert Half is a top brand name in the labor recruitment industry, which also runs one of the most conservative balance sheets for any business in America (dropped from the S&P 500 index in 2024). Versus an equity market cap around $2.6 billion today, the company held $2.1 billion in current assets (cash and receivables mostly) vs. $1.6 billion in total liabilities at the end of December 2025. Just $175 million in long-term building leases exists against "zero" financial debt with an interest component.

The good news is Wall Street analysts are forecasting sales to stabilize this year and begin growing again next year, with EPS rebounding smartly. If this proves correct, Robert Half is better than a sound value play today.

Let's run through the unique undervaluation setup, with short sellers piling into the stock during early 2026, pushing the price below what I feel is reasonable. The combination of rising short interest on a decent earnings report last week matched against deep value available to new buyers has me thinking Robert Half is sitting in a similarly bullish (countertrend) trading setup to Enphase Energy (ENPH). I discussed ENPH in January here, just before a +50% price spike, as a pick holding exceptional short squeeze potential.

Real Underlying Value For Your Capital

Seeking Alpha's Quant Factor Valuation Grade is an overall "A+" for Robert Half. My view is just a stabilization in sales and profits could allow for a sizeable rebound in the share quote.

The standout valuation discount to me is the EV to sales datapoint. Basically, the stock is trading at its lowest ratio of 0.44x since the depths of the Great Recession in early 2009. The long-term average is 1.5x. So, if recruiting and hiring trends actually pick up in 2026, all kinds of share upside could be coiled like a spring.

The next most important idea to contemplate is the extraordinary dividend yield of 9.2% today - backstopped by around $460 million in cash on hand, and a super-strong balance sheet. Despite earnings in 2025 falling below the cash distribution payout, management has decided to keep the dividend flowing for now. Although coverage of the dividend is lacking on current 2026 income projections, assuming the business experiences a turnaround this year with better profitability, the $2.36 payout annually is easily supported for a number of years. Even with a flatline in operating profits, Robert Half could continue paying the dividend subsidized with cash holdings for 3-4 years, in my estimation.

Anyway, you can review the massive RHI yield spread vs. the equivalent S&P 500 dividend of 1.06% currently. Until 2023, Robert Half never paid a rate far above the U.S. equity market average.

Short Interest As Future Price Support

Which brings me to the huge, short interest discussion. Shorts have to cover the 9.2% dividend on borrowed shares to maintain their position. Why not short a stock with a dividend yield of 1% or less? Thousands exist. It's an unnecessary carrying expense, meaning shorts are unlikely to remain near record level positioning for very long (in my opinion).

Any good news from the company will almost surely translate into a significant round of covering, propelling price higher quickly. That's the "short squeeze" story in a nutshell.

Dramatic short covering after the late January Q4 2025 earnings release lasted only one day, pushing the price from $27 to $35. Then, shorts piled back into Robert Half, dumping extra shares. Today's modern record of 17.3% of outstanding shares borrowed and sold into the marketplace (from 12.2% at the beginning of the year) is looking quite overdone. The related $1 or $2 change in price is rather ordinary vs. the spike in short selling, which is an excellent sign that long-term investors buying the stock are offsetting their selling.

The previous short position high around 10% of outstanding count in late 2019 helped to open a tremendous buy opportunity in Robert Half, especially after the COVID pandemic panic selling. Rising from $40 in March 2020 to $130 in early 2022 (+225% for a gain), short covering was a major support for the price rally (boxed in red below). Plus, the quote was still able to rise +120% from late 2019, dramatically outperforming the S&P 500 rise of +35% over this span. Something similar could be lurking in the years ahead if the operating business surprises with earnings beats and positive news.

Insider Transactions

Insiders and management have been net buyers of the stock through share compensation and options over the past 12 months. Plus, they have not sold any shares at prices under $35 over the past three months. If this group believed the company was in long-term trouble, vs. a slump lasting several years, I would expect to see high levels of liquidation.

Final Thoughts

To me, Robert Half is well positioned to ride out a recession with its A+ balance sheet for financial strength and conservatism. It's even possible a troubled jobs market will encourage desperate workers out of luck to "pay" recruiters to find steady employment. This new trend and potential source for added business revenue could be the saving grace for shareholders beginning this year. Robert Half could potentially collect fees from both workers and employers in the future!

Investment Risks

What are the investment risks? I would say the main risk I worry about is AI. If the tie-up between employees and employers can be done without Robert Half's middleman help, the company could be entering a prolonged downtrend right now. Yet, online tools and websites for matching/finding workers have been around for three decades. In this sense, quality vetted employees available at Robert Half could remain in demand.

Of course, stock market direction risk is something to consider for a Robert Half position, as with any other equity investment presently. If we are on the verge of a recession and bear market in stocks, RHI may be stuck around $30 all year.

My Conclusion

For a rating, I am confident the immediate and obvious pros outweigh the potential long-term cons of owning Robert Half. The short position could be setting itself up for disappointment. If this is true, a short squeeze may vastly increase price, as share demand could overwhelm available supply each day. I believe weak-hand shareholders have sold over the past few years (on the steady downtrend), while the leftover group of owners can be described as strong-hand holders not willing to sell for a profit of several dollars per share. This setup screams to me a monster imbalance of buying vs. selling is approaching, sooner or later.

My reversion-to-the-mean price target, in terms of the valuation, using the 5-year average P/E of 20x EPS on $2.30 in 2027 estimates, gets you to $46 in 12 to 18 months. Such a move would deliver a total return of +85%, including the dividend payout.

Downside risk may be limited to $20 this year. Price to tangible book value (on mostly current assets) is already a modern-record low in early 2026 at 2.6x. As long as the company can generate a decent level of income, I doubt this number will fall below 2.0x on a sustainable basis.

I own a growing stake in Robert Half and have a 12-month Buy rating, outperformance outlook vs. the S&P 500. My summary is purchasing shares under $30 should generate nice trading/investing profits by the end of 2026, if not by summertime.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: BHP: Decent Quarterly Results Tied To Copper, But Demand May Disappoint (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis.

BHP Group Limited (BHP) reported its fiscal year 2026 Q2 results, and by most metrics, we saw continued improvement. However, with a significant share price gain of over 40% in the past year, much of the improvement we saw is now arguably baked into its share price and valuation. This is especially the case, given that there are signs that copper demand, which has been a key driver in BHP's financial results, may see some short-term weakness going forward.

I see BHP continuing to do well in the longer term, on its copper segment outlook, with a decent chance that other segments of the company's mining portfolio will surprise to the upside as well. However, this year the copper market may see some weakness, as I already stated. Its share price & valuation are now such that I see only limited upside from here, especially if I am right about the copper market headwinds. I sold my BHP shares, and I am now looking to initiate a position again, targeting a sizable dip in its share price before I start buying incrementally.

Going from a Buy to a Hold

I last covered BHP in November, when I stated my intention to start incrementally building a position in this company. Shortly after my initial investment in this stock, its share price started to rise, gaining well over 30%, at which point I decided to sell my position. My long-term goal is still in place. Considering the company's financial performance, as well as external factors, I intend to wait for a 15%-20% pullback in its share price from current levels, at which point I feel that the stock's valuation will be priced for more significant upside.

Copper prices boost BHP's financial performance

For the first half of fiscal year 2026, which ended at the end of December of last year, BHP saw revenues come in at $27.9 billion, an increase of almost 11% compared with the same time in the prior fiscal year. Profits attributable to BHP shareholders came in at $5.64 billion, up over 27% compared with the same period in the prior fiscal year. Profits rose twice as fast as revenues.

The significant increase in copper prices (HG1:COM) in 2025 had a major impact on BHP's financial performance.

As we can see, revenues derived from its copper segment accounted for roughly half of all revenues for the first half of fiscal year 2026.

BHP's financial results going forward are clearly tied to a great extent to the global copper market for the foreseeable future. That is not to say that iron ore, as well as coal, emerging segments such as potash, silver, and so on, are not also important to the company's future outlook.

Debt increased by just over $2 billion, to $24.6 billion in the first half of fiscal year 2026, compared with the same period from the prior year, which is reflected in higher interest costs for the same period. Financial costs came in at $973 million for the first half of fiscal year 2026, which was a 25% Y-O-Y increase. Interest costs made up 3.5% of revenues, which is fairly close to the 5% threshold where I see interest costs taking up too much of a company's internal resources, risking a financial vicious cycle trap. It is moving in the wrong direction, toward that threshold, at a seemingly fast pace, which is something I am watching for to see if it will change in the coming quarters.

The copper market may face some headwinds going forward

The last time I covered BHP, I highlighted the company's well-positioned portfolio to take advantage of growing demand for commodities such as copper & iron, mostly driven by fast-growing developing nations such as India, Indonesia, Vietnam, and others. Since then, certain signs point to global copper demand potentially disappointing this year, even if the longer-term dynamics still point to robust demand growth.

Global EV sales growth is softening. January sales were down 3% Y-O-Y based on registration data. The implications for copper demand are both direct and indirect. EVs need about 3-4 times more copper input compared with manufacturing conventional cars. Fewer EV sales also mean less of a need to expand global electric power generation and transmission infrastructure, which indirectly also impacts copper demand negatively.

Copper demand growth from grid infrastructure building may also be negatively impacted by a slower-than-expected surge in AI data centers being built. My SA colleague Jeremy Bloom wrote an interesting article suggesting that fewer than expected AI data centers may be built. Resulting copper demand growth may have been overestimated, as is the case with EV sales growth forecasts.

As we can see, at least for now, JPMorgan (JPM) sees continued strength in copper prices this year. The outlook for BHP is bolstered accordingly. If copper prices start heading in the opposite direction due to less robust demand growth than expected, this company might have a very hard time duplicating the revenue and profit growth it achieved last year.

Other segments of the company's mining assets portfolio could potentially make up for potential weakness in the copper market. Iron ore (SCO:COM) in particular may surprise to the upside if demand forecasts are pushed higher due to developing nations looking to build out their infrastructure as well as industrialize.

The fact that iron market expectations are for very moderate growth means that it would not take much of a boost in demand forecasts for the iron ore market to unexpectedly rally.

Investment implications.

BHP is currently trading at a forward P/E ratio of 16, which is up significantly from under 13 when I last covered this stock and invested accordingly. Whereas its dividend yield was over 4% when I bought shares in November, the yield is now down to under 3%, making it far less attractive as a long-term investment in this regard as well.

A peer comparison of BHP's stock performance, valuation, and dividend shows that alternative mining investments may offer higher upside potential.

Even though Vale's (VALE) stock outperformed BHP in the past year, it still has a far lower forward P/E ratio at just 7.4, while it also has a 7% dividend. Vale's mined output is mostly iron ore, which is the main reason why it is trading at such a steep discount relative to BHP. Copper is thought to have more upside. As I pointed out already, there is a chance that market fundamentals will favor iron producers over copper-focused companies this year. Given these considerations, I currently see Vale stock as a better buy, with BHP in need of a significant pullback before the valuation justifies the risk/reward implications, as I laid them out.

Risks to my thesis.

Having sold my position in BHP with the intent of waiting for a more opportune re-entry, there is always the risk that I will miss out. Many factors could drive copper prices much higher, and BHP's share price with it. We could even see a broader spike in commodity prices, including iron ore, coal, and BHP's more marginal mined products. With the Federal Reserve striving to push interest rates lower and many other factors, I can see a potential outcome where a rush to secure tangible assets that are increasingly priced in currencies that are being devalued through excessive debt issuance, as well as accommodating monetary policies, could ignite a rally in all mining stocks. I do have ample exposure to the mining sector, especially oil producers and gold miners. I would not miss out entirely by not having a position in BHP stock.

Conclusions

The latest financial results affirm BHP's close performance correlation with the copper market. Though it has a robust iron ore segment and many other assets that provide it exposure to other mined commodities, they will not drive this company's financial performance to as great an extent as copper will. As I pointed out, there are reasons to expect that global copper demand growth will be slower than expected; therefore, BHP's stock might not perform as well this year as some might expect. I expect that there will be sizable dips in its share price, which is where I see a buying opportunity, where this year is the year to build a longer-term position, to take advantage of longer-term global demand trends.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Critical Metals Corp.: At The Nexus Of Geopolitics And Heavy Rare Earths Demand
data.description:
Earlier this week, Critical Metals Corp. (CRML) reported the results of the latest drilling at its Tanbreez mine in Greenland. The numbers were impressive: with thirty-three holes bored, the “total rare earth oxide” (TREO) results averaged .44%, with peak assays providing an astounding .94%.

These outsized results are precisely what makes this obscure micro-cap so provocative as a long-term investment thesis. Unknown to virtually all investors ten months ago, CRML is quietly becoming a central element in the DoD’s ongoing “strategic re-shoring effort,” one that gives Western militaries a way to rebuild their own HREE supply chain outside the "long arm jurisdiction" of Beijing.

The Company

Headquartered in midtown Manhattan, Critical Metals Corp was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands and commenced public trading less than two years ago. The firm is focused on two prospective mining operations --one in Austria, the other in Greenland:

  • The Wolfsberg Lithium Project (near Graz) which hopes to supply key lithium products to the European market.
  • Tanbreez, its declared “flagship project,” just north of Qaqortoq.

The Wolfsberg project aims to become a primary lithium resource for the European auto industry. The project has a major tie-up with BMW, and another with a Saudi venture that is building a lithium hydroxide refinery in the Kingdom. The EU has stated it wants to “stand up” domestic strategic mineral producers, and this mine is centrally located for any European distribution, well-connected via rail and highway.

For most of 2025, Wolfsberg looked fully “greenlighted,” with no additional environmental assessment needed by the regional government. However, that hit a snag in December, when a federal level ruling asked for more disclosures. This action will likely delay a 2027-2028 commercialization, though Wolfsberg will still likely be the first lithium miner operating in the EU.

While Wolfberg slows, the Tanbreez project accelerates.

A remarkable number of developments have progressed since late summer, and the speed should pick up this Spring. It is important to note that until very recently CRML was only an indirect owner with a 42% stake in Tanbreez, with Rimbal Pty Ltd, through a subsidiary, holding the controlling 50.5% stake. That changed in October when the company declared it has a commanding 92.5% stake, having offered 14.5 million shares to Rimbal. In early January, CRML stated that its buildout of a pilot facility at the mine would be “in use by May 2026” and that they had bought a corporate location in Qaqortoq itself.

Why all this hurried activity?

The Tanbreez mine is a behemoth, exceptional like Saudi Arabia’s Ghawar field or Chile’s Escondida in their respective categories (oil and copper). It is arguably the largest REE deposit site ever found, with nearly 45 million tons of material (at .38 - .48% TREO). An early preliminary economic assessment of the site valued it at $2.8-$3.6 billion NPV, with an initial commercial output of 85,000 tons per annum (tpa), expanding up to 425,000.

That is likely to go up.

Tanbreez and Its Strategic Metals

More importantly for investors, 27% of the mine's ore haul is heavy rare earths (HREEs). This is a critical differentiator. HREEs are the ones truly critical for US defense needs, being in higher demand due to their role in military-grade systems and ultra-high precision engineering.

China’s control of the HREE market is at least 95%, compared to 60% of light rare earths (LREEs). The US has helped stand up MP’s Mountain Pass mine, but that is mostly LREEs.

In early April and again in mid-October 2025, Beijing introduced strict controls, licensing requirements, and “long-arm jurisdiction” on nine HREEs as well as downstream magnets aimed at US defense contractors, with yttrium, dysprosium, terbium, samarium, & gadolinium being the specific metals named.

By 2028, Tanbreez’s immense HREE trove should allow the West to “slip the geostrategic noose” that Deng Xiaoping had set in place in the late 80s.

As I described in an earlier article, this is mission-critical. In conjunction with processors like UURAF or USAR, CRML provides a credible long-term Western alternative to China’s utter domination of the HREE market, providing refiners in the US, Europe, and even Saudi Arabia a stream of resource.

Also auspicious: the mine is located at the southern, less icy lip of Greenland with full year shipping possibilities:

HREE Defense Applications

What are some of the military uses that these metals are used for? The following list provides a cursory description:

  • Yttrium – used in high-temperature jet engine coatings, its thermal barrier properties also enhance weapon precision and protect the superalloys installed in the F-35, for example.
  • Dysprosium –used in missile guidance systems (surface-to-air, cruise, anti-ship) dues to its high operational temperatures. Used in sonar and underwater detection.
  • Terbium – used in high-temperature permanent magnets. Tb-based green phosphors deployed in laser and targeting assignments, as well as night-vision displays.
  • Samarium -- Samarium-cobalt magnets are super high-temperature resistant and help guidance systems and smart bombs. China controls nearly the entire global supply.
  • Europium -- Used in nuclear control rods and to warm up LED display systems.

In addition, the mine offers supplies of these strategic elements:

Gallium --Because of its resistance to radiation, it is deployed in satellites. It is also now used to semiconductors, tiny electronics and in electronic warfare –jammers, etc—due to its high-power efficiencies. Last year China imposed and then retracted an export ban aimed at US defense contractors.

Zirconium –Because of its corrosion resistance it is used in hypersonic missiles.

Hafnium –its superior neutron absorption makes it expectation for rocket nozzles and cladding nuclear rods in submarines.

Niobium –Capable of holding its structural integrity above 2,400 degrees Celsius, it is used for hypersonic, rocketry, and defense-related superconducting.

Tanbreez has a big supply of these various elements, as disclosed in a recent presentation:

Relationships

The proverbial Gretzky puck is moving fast with rare earths, and CRML’s various tie-ups in the US, Europe, and the Middle East are suggestive of just how global that demand for a new REE supply chain has become.

In late August, CRML signed a letter of intent for a ten-year off-take arrangement to supply 10,000 tons of HREE concentrate annually to Ucore’s DoD-funded refining facility in Louisiana. (Ucore (UURAF) failed at mining HREEs at its Alaska site and has since shifted its focus to its novel refining processes.) This was a sizable win for the CRML, and it sent the ticker into the spotlight.

In December, the firm unveiled a 50-50 JV with a Romanian state-owned entity with uranium experience to set up a processing facility and secure long-term off-take rights. A month later, the firm signed a non-binding term sheet for a 50-50 JV with a big Saudi mining outfit, which wants to take 25% of Tanbreez’s total capacity for its facilities in the Kingdom.

Things are clearly moving fast, despite the fact the mine itself is still a hypothetical.

Financials

CRML is a profitless company with no revenue. It incurred a $13.1 million operating income loss in its June quarter. Those expenses are likely to increase as the firm proceeds through a build-out of the pilot plant, storage facilities, and then the larger mine. The coming quarter will also see the dilution from the 42% to 98% stake control it got last fall.

The dilution is well worth it. With a present value of $3.0 billion, Tanbreez is a crown jewel, altering the potentialities of the West much like the Potesi mine did for 16th century Spain or the Anaconda Copper mine for America’s early electrification efforts. It has had outstanding HREE drilling results confirmed by independent labs with purity levels that are aerospace level. In addition, it has discovered a commercially viable gallium supply, which goes up in value with each restrictive action by Beijing.

In terms of financing, the firm secured an EXIM bank letter of intent for $120 million. It has completed an $85 million PIPE. In terms of clients, 100% of their proposed mining has been now covered by off-take agreements –the Romanian JV with 50%, Saudi with 25%, REalloys with 15%, Ucore with 10%.

Their Romanian JV takes them "up the value chain" and will be –according to management-- Europe’s first mine-to-magnet supply, offering vertical integration a la the Chinese model. Best yet for CRML shareholder: the tie-up delivers no dilution, with the Romanian government carrying their 50% interest in the refinery. They receive 50% of downstream value with 0% dilution or capital outlays.

This may because the EU has designated 3.5 billion euros to develop its own rare earth supply chain projects, and Romania knows this JV is well positioned to get some of that funding, thereby positioning itself closer to the continent’s high-tech manufacturers.

In its latest corporate presentation, management laid out Tanbreez’s revenue and cost trajectory in broad strokes:

The net present value is assessed at $3 billion over the course of a 25-year mine life, extracting around 37.5 million tons of ore. They estimate free cash flow kicking in around year four (of the 25 year timeline), which probably synchs with the commencement of phase 2 period (see table).

Technicals

At present, the stock trades at $9.05 (with a market cap of $975 million). That is up substantially from its $2.75 price a year ago, but well down from its frothy $29 last October.

Looking at the charts, we see a stock that is no longer technically “overbought.” In fact, the slow stochastic and MACD indicators suggest the ticker is trending toward oversold. At $9.05, the ticker is below its 20-day and 50-day averages but not yet hitting its 200-day of $7.69.

I would get constructive as it approaches the $8.50 range, where there have been two “gap-ups” (mid Oct. and early Jan.). I also see the $7.50 -$7.75 range as being very opportune as we saw support levels there in Nov-Dec and it has become the 200-day average.

Because the stock is so responsive to news, investors should not overpay but also to be sanguine about your entry points –the Nov-Dec support line was clearly above the Sept-Oct support line, probably as the stock became more understood by investors (and actively speculated on by day traders). If the pilot mine comes online in April, i.e. earlier than expected, the stock will likely pop on the news.

Risks

The risks of investing in Critical Metal Corp. are myriad. This is an unproven company entering into two big mining projects that require massive expenditure to get operationally up and running. There is no guarantee that many of the “friendshoring” /nearshoring” policies instituted by the present US administration to stand up a domestic REE supply won’t be overturned by the next. Also, rare earth prices fluctuate widely due to manipulation and political risk, as we saw last year and during Beijing’s kerfuffle with Japan in 2010, and new innovations can reduce the demand for specific minerals. Besides China, there are many NATO-centered competitors springing up in the space - some backed by the US, some by the EU - though none have the lodestone heft of Tanbreez.

Conclusion

Any investment in CRML should be a small position, with the understanding that it is a very speculative bet on a particular view of the future. As I have explored earlier, we have entered a new era of military science and technology, one that is based on autonomous systems and “mass precision.” That means "quantity and quality" and a deep bench of "attritable" weaponry, all of which ironically need HREEs.

CRML is essentially a call option on one solution to an emerging geopolitical situation. It is a bet that “de-globalization” will not be reversed and that "peer-competitor rivalries” at both ends of the Eurasia landmass are not about to melt away but rather intensify this decade.

At present, I consider the ticker a HOLD, and will look for entries between $7.69 and $8.50.

data.title: Hycroft Mining: Up 1,300% Since My Sell Call - Why I'm Still Bearish
data.description:
Hycroft Mining Update

Way back in January 2023, I published, "AMC’s Hycroft Investment: A Sequel To A Nightmare For Investors" and rated HYMC a Sell, and that has turned out to be a painfully wrong call.

My thesis at the time was straightforward: Hycroft had a massive but uneconomic gold resource, falling production, and the company was lighting cash on fire (burning ~$5 million a month); the AMC investment felt like a meme-stock sideshow, not a strategic lifeline.

I valued the company at roughly $20 million, or basically net cash plus a small optionality premium, against a market cap of about $148 million at the time.

So, what happened?

The stock was trading around $3.00 (split-adjusted). Today, as I write this, HYMC trades near $42, up roughly 1,300%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 has returned approximately 87%. Gold itself has surged from about $1,896/oz to above $5,000, or 166% higher, since my last coverage. This was a horrible call by any measure, plain and simple.

I’m going to own this call. If you shorted HYMC based on my article, you got hurt (although I certainly didn’t recommend shorting then). That’s on me. In the gold sector, I’ve had far more winners than losers, but this one belongs firmly in the loss column (for now, at least).

But price and thesis are two different things. And as much as the chart has moved against my bearish call, it's worth asking the follow-up question: has the fundamental story actually changed, or has a rising gold tide simply lifted a boat that still has no engine?

HYMC has always attracted retail buyers. This is, after all, the stock that AMC Entertainment decided to invest in, and in a market where gold is rising and speculative appetite is running high, momentum alone can do a lot of heavy lifting.

The Resource Is Big, That's Not the Point

On February 18, Hycroft released some interesting news: An updated mineral resource estimate showing 16.4 million gold ounces and 562.6 million silver ounces (measured and indicated), approximately 55% higher than the 2023 estimate. The company also delineated an initial high-grade silver resource of 90.2 million ounces (measured, indicated) at the Brimstone and Vortex systems. The stock surged 21% on this news.

I’ll give credit where it’s due: the resource growth is real, and the high-grade silver intercepts, including intervals exceeding 2,000 g/t Ag, are genuinely interesting from a geological standpoint.

But the thing I keep coming back to is that a big resource has always been the Hycroft story. Hycroft’s challenge has never been the size of its deposit, in my opinion. Its problem is the economics of actually extracting it. It's a complex and capital-intensive processing pathway that has yet to be validated in a full economic study. That was the case 3 years ago, and it is still the case, as far as I'm aware.

Metallurgical test work has shown recoveries of approximately 83% for gold and 78% for silver using a milling and POX flowsheet. Those are decent numbers. But recoveries are just one input. We still don’t know what the upfront capital cost looks like, what the operating costs will be, or what the project’s NPV actually is. Those are the numbers that matter, and we don’t have them yet (although the PEA is coming soon).

Still No Production, Still No Revenue, Still Burning Cash

Let’s step back and state the obvious: Hycroft Mining isn't mining anything. The company has zero revenue. It has zero operating cash flow. It has been burning cash for years.

To the company’s credit, management has improved the balance sheet. Hycroft raised approximately $235 million through equity offerings in 2025 and used that capital to repay all outstanding debt (approximately $136 million), making the company debt-free for the first time as a public entity. The company has about $200 million in cash, according to its corporate presentation.

That sounds nice until you remember how they got there. They diluted shareholders extensively.

See the chart below. Shares have roughly doubled over the past year (from 42.5M to 91M). Most of this share count increase came from the Sept 2025 private placement (14M units) and Oct 2025 underwritten offering (23M+ shares at $6.50). The company has been aggressive in raising capital, and then spending it on studies and exploration.

And even with ~$200 million in the bank today, I think the path to production is at least 4–5 years away (assuming a positive technical report).

If you’re building a mine the right way, you need a PEA, then a pre-feasibility study, then a full feasibility study, then permitting, then financing, then construction. It looks like Hycroft is still at Step 1. That’s a long runway of cash burn with no revenue to speak of for at least several years.

The Valuation Problem

I think Hycroft would be a much more interesting stock at a much lower valuation. Unfortunately, Hycroft currently commands a market capitalization of roughly $3.5 billion. For context:

Metric

Value

Market Cap

~$3.5 billion

Revenue (TTM)

$0

Operating Cash Flow (TTM)

~($30 million annually)

Cash Position (Q1 2026)

$200 million

Shares Outstanding

99.2 million (fully diluted)

Estimated Time to Production

4–5 years (at best)

Sources: HYMC Q3 2025 10-Q, Stock Information page

You’re essentially paying a $3.3 billion enterprise value for a company that doesn’t produce anything and won’t for years (most likely). I feel that the entire investment case rests on the belief that the upcoming PEA will show a project NPV large enough to justify this valuation.

In my view, that means you’d need to see something like a $3-5+ billion NPV at spot metal prices with upfront capex of $1 billion or less (or numbers somewhere in that range). That’s a high bar for a project of this scale and complexity.

And even if the PEA comes in strong, remember what it is: a Preliminary Economic Assessment. It’s the lowest level of economic study in mining, based on inferred resources that can’t be used in feasibility studies, with accuracy ranges of +/–50%.

Trust me, as someone who has now invested in this sector for 10+ years, PEA studies are not reliable. Countless projects have shown strong PEAs only to fall apart at the pre-feasibility or feasibility stage when costs are refined, and engineering gets real. A good PEA is a starting point, but not much more.

Why I'm Not Shorting It Either

There's a decent amount of interest in shorting here, with 8.33% of its shares short. Here’s where I might surprise some readers. Despite everything I’ve said, I wouldn't short HYMC right now (though I'm certainly tempted).

The reason is simple: I’m bullish on the metals. If gold runs to $7,000/oz and silver to $100–$150/oz—which I don’t think is out of the question in this cycle—speculative names like Hycroft could see further gains regardless of fundamentals. What if the U.S. decides to attack Iran, for example? Gold could rise significantly under this one scenario.

The stock also has strong momentum, a loyal retail following, and support from investor Eric Sprott, and near-term catalysts in the form of additional high-grade silver drill results and the PEA itself.

Shorting a momentum stock in a raging gold bull market is a recipe for pain, even if you’re right on the fundamentals.

Getting back to Sprott, he's one of the most respected names in precious metals investing. He happens to hold over 40% of HYMC’s outstanding shares and has been actively adding to his position, buying 200,000 shares as recently as January 29 (worth $9.2 million). Maybe Sprott knows something I don't.

When Sprott puts that kind of money behind a name, it deserves attention. I respect his track record. After all, he was one of the reasons I first invested in Kirkland Lake Gold years ago (it was a 10-bagger for him and a multi-bagger for myself). On this one, though, I’m more than happy to sit it out and watch from the sidelines.

Hycroft Mining: What Would Change My Mind

There’s a scenario where Hycroft becomes a real company with real economics. Here’s what I’d need to see to reverse my SELL call:

  • Strong technical results. A PEA showing a project NPV north of $5 billion at spot metal prices, with upfront capex of $1 billion or less (or at least something within reason). With about ~$200 million in the bank, Hycroft would need significant debt financing and additional equity dilution to build this mine (which, sadly, is nothing new for this company), but a manageable capex number would make the financing conversation much easier.
  • Confirmation that the processing flowsheet works, not just in lab tests. Pressure oxidation and roasting are proven technologies, but applying them to Hycroft’s specific ore body at commercial throughput is a different challenge.
  • A clear permitting and construction timeline that makes the 4–5 year path to production feel real and de-risked, rather than a pipe dream, and perhaps even shortens the production timeline, would be bullish for the stock.

I’m obviously not convinced the capex will come in that low, nor the NPV that high. But I’m open to being wrong again. The data will tell the story.

Where I'd Put The Money Instead

If you’re looking for speculative gold exposure with real catalysts and a clearer path to value creation, I’d point you toward two names I think offer better risk/reward at the moment. Neither of these stocks looks necessarily cheap at the moment. But, both are also interesting takeover targets in my view.

  • Perpetua Resources (PPTA) is building the Stibnite Gold Project in Idaho, with ~4.8 million ounces of reserves, expected to produce around 450,000 oz/year in the first four years. It also hosts the only domestic antimony reserve in the country, which has strategic defense value. They've already broken ground on early works, selected contractors, and are pursuing a $2 billion financing package.

  • Integra Resources (ITRG) is a promising gold junior focused on developing its DeLamar Gold-Silver project, located in the mining-friendly jurisdiction of Idaho. The feasibility study was just released, showing a 1.8-year payback and strong NPV-to-capex ratio. This is a late-stage, construction-ready project in my view. And, unlike Hycroft, Integra actually produces gold, as its Florida Canyon mine delivered roughly 71,000 ounces in 2025, providing cash flow to fund development of DeLamar. With a 10 million ounce gold-equivalent resource base across three projects and federal permitting commencing in 2026, Integra offers pretty nice upside in a tier-one jurisdiction.

Both of these are U.S.-based projects with clear development timelines and near-term catalysts.

Bottom Line: Sell

Was I wrong on my original call back in 2023 and earlier? On the stock price, absolutely. A 1,300% move against my call is about as wrong as it gets. I’m not going to hide from that.

But on the fundamentals? I’d argue very little has changed. Hycroft still doesn’t mine gold. It still doesn’t generate revenue. It’s still years away from production. The resource has gotten bigger, but bigger doesn’t mean economic. And the market is currently pricing this company at $3.3 billion on the hope that a preliminary study will validate what the stock price already assumes.

Maybe the PEA will blow the doors off, and I’ll have to write a third article accepting failure yet again. I’ve been wrong before, and I’ll be wrong again. But at this valuation, for a pre-production exploration company, I think the risk/reward definitely leans more heavily to the downside. There are better places to put your money in this gold bull market.

data.title: Endangerment Finding And The XLU
data.description:
On 2/12/26 the EPA eliminated the 2009 endangerment finding with regard to greenhouse gas emissions. While the initial deregulation is aimed primarily at vehicle emissions, it has significant implications for the utilities sector ETF (XLU), which I last wrote about in September.

This is a highly politicized topic so I will start with my standard disclaimer. I will make no commentary on whether the deregulation is good or bad for America or the environment, and instead focus on the financial implications for the related companies. If I am writing correctly, the reader should have no idea what my personal opinions are. With that out of the way, let us examine how this may affect utilities.

Endangerment finding and power generation

In 2009, the EPA came out with an endangerment finding stipulating that certain greenhouse gases, including CO2, endanger public health. This ruling formed the basis for further regulation against CO2 emissions.

It proved to be quite impactful to power generation as the U.S. electric grid derives 60% of energy from fossil fuels. Most relevant to today’s discussion is the 16.2% of electricity that comes from coal, but natural gas is affected as well.

Using similar endangerment concepts, the G7 countries agreed to phase out coal by 2030-2035 and/or capture emissions.

The U.S. was following a similar schedule with a 2024 EPA ruling under Biden administration forcing coal plants to either retire or capture emissions.

As capturing emissions is quite expensive relative to the value of power, many coal plants and some other CO₂-emitting plants planned retirement. The following plants were scheduled for retirement in 2025:

Since then, 2 factors have completely changed the dynamic:

  1. Demand for electricity has surged in a way that was previously not anticipated
  2. Trump was elected and has a very different stance on coal

Surge in electricity demand makes retirement more difficult to overcome

For decades, overall electricity demand was essentially flat. Slight increases to population and technology use were balanced out by energy efficiency improvements like LED lightbulbs.

However, as the AI infrastructure boom hits and data centers start guzzling electricity by the GW, aggregate demand will surge.

The EIA predicts significantly higher demand in 2026 and 2027 with further acceleration likely beyond that.

This changes the calculus on coal plant retirements.

If, for example, the grid merely had to replace the 16% of generation coming from coal as the plants retired over the next 10 years, that is an entirely doable task.

However, it now seems as though the grid will have to build vast amounts of incremental generation. It is quite challenging to increase capacity by that much while also trying to retire such a large portion of existing capacity.

New administration has pro-coal policies

The Trump administration has a much friendlier stance toward coal. Per the DOE Fact Sheet:

“The Trump Administration and DOE have issued 19 emergency orders to secure the U.S. power grid and prevent unnecessary power outages, including preventing the closure of five reliable coal power plants.”

Trump has prevented the closure of coal plants and presumably will remove mandates that are calling for future retirements.

It remains unknown how much of the 16% of electricity production by coal will remain, but it seems quite likely that a higher percentage will remain than previously anticipated.

So what does this mean for the XLU?

Implications of changed energy policy on XLU

The XLU ETF has the following top holdings.

It can largely be broken down into 2 groups:

1) Regulated utilities like NextEra Energy (NEE), Southern Company (SO), Duke (DUK), Dominion (D), and American Electric Power (AEP).

2) Independent power producers (IPPs) like Constellation (CEG), or Vistra (VST).

Regulated utilities are tasked with supplying affordable power to their jurisdictions. They build enough supply to meet demand. Each of the major regulated utilities has a massive pipeline through 2030. Some of them are forecasting incremental infrastructure investment equivalent to their current market caps.

If coal were to be phased out, the incremental build-out would be even larger. They would have to both replace lost power plants and service new demand. Higher incremental load would benefit the utilities because they get paid a certain regulated ROE on whatever they build. A larger rate base means more revenues at the same ROE which means higher overall earnings.

In that aspect, the move toward friendlier coal policies is a negative.

However, it is also the case that utilities often earn less than their allotted ROEs. To the extent that deregulation allows them to operate existing plants at lower cost, it could help them get up to their ROE caps.

Overall, I believe the Trump administration’s coal-friendlier stance is a net neutral for regulated utilities. The regulated utilities are poised for significant growth from the surge in demand. That will be the main driver with policy changes like this only having minor impacts and in this case, the positives and negatives of the change largely offset for the regulated utilities.

Independent power producers thrive in an environment of energy shortages. In a previous article on CEG we discussed how they were benefitting from the remarkably high PJM auction prices. Essentially, large plant retirements have been keeping PJM’s capacity rather tight.

Note above how the retirements basically cancel out capacity additions from 2019-2023. Also note that the vast majority of retirements were coal plants.

To the extent that the new EPA stance on endangerment makes coal plants stick around, PJM’s capacity will be less constrained. With more spare capacity, they will be less likely to have to pay egregiously high prices for incremental power.

CEG, VST and the other IPPs have been experiencing explosive earnings growth due to being able to sell power into PJM and other grids at these extraordinarily high prices.

Looser energy regulation facilitates more energy production and lower energy prices. The IPPs lose in that scenario.

They are perfectly fine companies and, in my opinion, will be fine longer term. The market prices, however, may have gotten too high in anticipating the extreme revenues to last much longer.

Summing up impacts on XLU

  • In my opinion, Coal-friendly policy is a significant negative for IPPs.
  • Coal-friendly policy is a net neutral for regulated utilities.

Overall, I view the EPA's 2/12/26 rescinding of the endangerment finding to be a negative for the XLU. That said, there is enough tailwind from surging demand that the sector can still perform well.

In my opinion, an investor would be wise to consider buying individual regulated utilities rather than an ETF like the XLU. This would afford exposure to the demand tailwinds while avoiding the overvaluation which is largely only present in the IPPs.

data.title: Babcock & Wilcox: Providing Speed-To-Market Options For AI Power Generation
data.description:
Thesis

Babcock and Wilcox (BW) has historically been a provider of coal fueled boiler technology. As one can imagine, this business has struggled in recent years.

However, the company also offers services for converting older power plants from coal to a natural gas fuel supply. Further, the company offers new-build natural gas boiler technology at a price point that is competitive with combined cycle natural gas power plants.

The drawback is likely to be overall plant efficiency, but this solution provides speed to market in ways that no utility can offer. In November, BW announced it had been granted a limited notice to proceed on a 1.2 GW power plant project as part of an AI datacenter project for Applied Digital (APLD). BW expects this plant to be operational in 2028, providing a project solution that gives the customer a product that is on time and on budget.

The contract for providing four natural gas fired boilers and associated steam turbines is valued at $1.5B. Based on historical EBITDA conversion rates, this project alone holds the potential to double BW’s EBITDA generation in 2027. Because this technology is proven and speed-to-market is a priority, I expect this solution to gain further traction in the data center realm.

I rate BW as a STRONG BUY candidate due to strong growth prospects and a stable balance sheet.

Exploring The Data Center Contract

Boiler technology is not new. Traditional coal plants pulverize the rock and inject the coal dust into a boiler to create steam and eventually spin a turbine-generator set. Retrofitting these boilers to use natural gas instead of coal has been something that has occurred quite frequently since the shale revolution. In fact, over 100 plants were converted from 2011-2019.

Now, as data center developers race for more power, the conversation turns to an all-of-the-above approach. Hyper-scalers need speed to market, and thus sticking to one standard approach doesn't fit. As a result, APLD has selected BW to provide four 300MW boiler systems to power their data center campus.

This selection represents a new entrant into a rapidly growing market. In this space we are also seeing modular gas fired engine-generator set projects being deployed in a modular fashion to supply behind the meter power. These engine solutions are in addition to the standard utility supply agreement for baseload power. Thus, it makes sense that boiler technology can also be deployed productively as long as it meets the criteria of readily available while also being cost competitive.

Speed To Market

First, let's discuss the time to market aspect. BW expects to have this 1.2 GW plant running in 2028. Even targeting a 2H startup, this puts this project around 2.5 years from completion.

By comparison, the lead time for engine components is approaching 90 weeks. This is a significant jump from 50 weeks just 6 months ago. Demand for these components has ramped up significantly for both power and natural gas compression. This does not include time for assembly or deployment.

The expanding backlog for engine components puts this technology in the same ballpark as BW's boilers in terms of time to market. Both of these technologies also have significantly shorter cycles than utility scale power plants that require extensive permitting and interconnection planning.

The real advantage BW brings to the table is the scale at which it can provide a fast solution for its customers. Natural gas reciprocating engines supplied by Caterpillar (CAT) come as large as 4.5 MW. In contrast, BW's standard boiler design will bring 300 MWs of power to the table. While a single engine unit may beat a BW boiler to the starting line by a few months, the difference in output is well worth the slightly longer timeline.

Cost Per KW Comparison

The most efficient power plant design is the combined cycle natural gas power plant. These are typically built on the scale of 1,000 MWs or larger while achieving thermal efficiency rates above 60%.

In the utility sector, grid operators use capacity auctions to attract investment in new infrastructure to meet the growing demand. As part of that auction process, grid operators such as PJM develop what is considered the Cost Of New Entry or CONE.

This CONE value is used as an input to capacity auctions. Ultimately, that input plays a part in determining future year capacity prices that power providers will receive. In the most recent analysis, it has been determined that the CONE for a 2028 deployable combined cycle plant is $1,929/KW for total installed costs.

Now let's take that number and compare it to the two other alternatives discussed so far. Compression providers such as Kodiak Gas Services (KGS) are reporting engine costs of approximately $1,200 per HP or $1,600/KW. Again, these engines are used in both compression and in generator applications as the drive train is interchangeable.

Because of the close-knit relationship between these two businesses, KGS has invested in developing its own power business. In early February, KGS acquired Distributed Power Solutions in a $675m deal. This deal allowed KGS to acquire 384MWs of deployable power in the form of Caterpillar reciprocating engines and turbines. At this transaction price, KGS paid $1,758 per kilowatt which is still cheaper than a large-scale power plant.

Turning to BW's boiler technology, we can see that the company has offered 1.2 GWs for a price of $1.5 billion. This translates into $1,250/kw, making it the most competitive price for rapid power deployment.

This should be a key selling point for data center providers. As shown below, the demand for electricity is expected to grow by 25% by 2030. BW appears to have a cost-effective solution to meet that market need.

Implications For EBITDA Growth And Valuation

BW is on track to deliver $600 million in revenue during 2025. As a result, a $1.5B contract is a big deal for this company.

In 2026, the company expects to generate $77.5m in EBITDA at the mid-point of guidance. This represents over 70% growth for the base business compared to annualized 2025 levels. This initial guidance does not include any gains from the proposed data center deal, so any additional earnings derived from this project is upside.

To estimate the total impact of this project we can look at historical EBITDA margin for the company. As a whole, BW has produced an EBITDA margin of 9.3%. However, this number is slightly higher for the company's Thermal division generated a margin of 11.6% YTD.

Thus, a $1.5B contract for the Thermal division should generate approximately $175m in EBITDA growth. Assuming this value is split evenly across 2027 and 2028, EBITDA is positioned for significant growth.
